mnemonics: SCID: Schema is the Design, Instance is the Data. Imagine a blueprint (Schema) and the finished building (Instance) filled with people and furniture (Data).
MAPS: Mapping between schemas ensures Application Program independence and Physical data independence.
PILE: Physical level is about storage (Hardware), Logical level is about structure (Software), View level is what the User sees.
keyPoints: A Database Schema is the logical structure (design/blueprint) of the database.
A Database Instance is the actual data (content/state) stored at a particular moment in time.
The Three-Schema Architecture separates the database into Internal (physical), Conceptual (logical), and External (view) levels.
Logical Data Independence: Immunity of application programs to changes in the conceptual schema (e.g., adding a new table).
Physical Data Independence: Immunity of the conceptual schema to changes in the internal schema (e.g., changing to a new storage device).
commonMistakes: Confusing Schema (design) with Instance (data). A schema change (e.g., adding a column) is structural, while an instance change (e.g., adding a row) is a data update.
Thinking Logical and Physical Data Independence are the same. Logical protects applications from logical changes; Physical protects the logical layer from physical storage changes.
Believing the 'View' level in the three-schema architecture is only for end-users. It is also crucial for application programs, providing each with its own tailored data perspective.
